claim: Hand A's HIGH class (top 146 types, cumulative 50.1% token coverage, from H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-01) exhibits word-frequency distribution properties matching published Basque text statistics: Zipf exponent in the 0.9-1.1 range, top-10 token share 15-25%, and rank-1 to rank-10 frequency ratio of 4-6x. Matching all three would support the Basque-like interpretation at the vocabulary-frequency level.
evidence_for: Natural languages uniformly exhibit Zipfian frequency distributions, but the exponent varies across languages and text registers. Basque corpora (Euskaltzaindia, Ereduzko Prosa Gaur) show ZIPF exponents typically 0.9-1.1 for narrative text — a narrower band than English (~1.0) or Latin (~0.8-0.9).

Hand A's HIGH class was characterised in H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-01 and H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-NLREF-01; the 50.1%-by-146-types compression is comparable to Basque's high-frequency-word compression due to rich case-marked function morphology.

Basque's top-10 highest-frequency words are dominated by function words (izan, ukan auxiliaries; eta 'and'; hau/hori/hura demonstratives; eta conjunctions). Their combined share in Basque narrative text typically runs 15-25% of tokens — a range Hand A's HIGH class top-10 can be compared against.
evidence_against: Natural-language frequency distributions are broadly similar (all Zipfian); distinguishing Basque from other agglutinative non-harmonic candidates by Zipf parameters alone is coarse-grained.

Hand A's HIGH class contains ambiguously-defined tokens (some may be abbreviated compound forms, some may be genuinely single morphemes). Direct comparison to Basque word types assumes comparable tokenisation.

Published Basque frequency statistics come from modern-Basque corpora; 15th-c. Basque would have had different high-frequency vocabulary (fewer Spanish-influenced loans; different auxiliary paradigm) and the frequency distribution may shift accordingly.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: Hand A full corpus, all tokens (not filtered to N>=3).

  HIGH CLASS (LOCKED): top 146 types by frequency, cumulative 50.1% of tokens. Identical definition to H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-01.

  METRICS:

  Z1 — ZIPF EXPONENT:
    Compute Zipf exponent alpha via least-squares fit of log(frequency) vs log(rank) on the HIGH class types only. Formula: log(f_r) = C - alpha * log(r), fit on ranks 1..146.
    Basque-compatible range: alpha in [0.85, 1.15] (slightly widened from [0.90, 1.10] to account for fit noise on 146 data points).

  Z2 — TOP-10 TOKEN SHARE:
    Compute sum of counts of top-10 Hand-A types as a fraction of total Hand-A tokens.
    Basque-compatible range: [0.15, 0.30] (slightly widened to account for register variation).

  Z3 — RANK-1 / RANK-10 FREQUENCY RATIO:
    Ratio of count(rank-1) / count(rank-10).
    Basque-compatible range: [3.5, 7.0] (slightly widened from [4, 6]).

  DIAGNOSTIC (non-decision-feeding):
    Also report: rank-1 / rank-20 ratio; fraction of HIGH types that are 1-glyph tokens (Basque function words are often short 2-4 letter words like 'eta', 'hau', 'bat'); mean length of top-10 HIGH types.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: all 3 metrics fall within Basque-compatible ranges.
    MARGINAL: 2 of 3 match.
    REFUTED: 0-1 of 3 match.

  CONTROL REFERENCE (informational): Hand A's parameters under the NOMENCLATOR-NLREF-01 comparison against Latin Vulgate and Italian Dante (3-of-5 head/tail measures showed Hand A more extreme than either reference). Note whether the current result is consistent with the NLREF observation or contradicts it.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if 3/3 metrics match Basque-compatible ranges. MARGINAL if 2/3. REFUTED if 0-1/3.

tests_run.details:
MARGINAL 2/3 under locked rule. Hand A's frequency distribution is partially compatible with Basque but fails on the most diagnostic metric (Zipf exponent).

  RESULTS (n=11022 Hand A tokens, 3513 unique types, HIGH_N=146 covering 50.06%):
    Z1 Zipf exponent:       0.7513  FAIL  (range [0.85, 1.15]; Hand A is SHALLOWER than Basque)
    Z2 top-10 token share:  0.1513  PASS  (range [0.15, 0.30]; at lower bound)
    Z3 rank-1/rank-10:      4.7216  PASS  (range [3.5, 7.0]; comfortably in range)

  Top-10 Hand A types: daiin, chol, chor, s, y, shol, or, ol, chy, dy.
    Mean length: 2.80 glyph-units.
    1-glyph types in HIGH class: 6/146 = 4.1%.

  INTERPRETATION:
    The Zipf exponent of 0.75 is substantially flatter than the 0.9-1.1 typical of Basque narrative text. A flatter Zipf means the top words don't dominate as strongly — Hand A's lexicon is more evenly distributed. This is the SAME phenomenon Brain-V identified in H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-NLREF-01, where Hand A was shown to be more extreme than both Latin Vulgate and Italian Dante on head/tail compression measures.

    The short-function-word test is particularly telling: in natural languages with rich morphology (Basque, Latin, Greek), the top-10 words are dominated by short function morphemes (2-3 chars: eta, hau, da / et, in, ad / καί, τοῦ, ὁ). Hand A's top-10 has mean length 2.80 but only 6/146 HIGH types are 1-glyph — meaning short function words are NOT characteristic of Hand A's highest-frequency class. Types like 'daiin' (5 chars), 'chol' (4), 'chor' (4), 'shol' (4) are MORPHOLOGICALLY-COMPLEX looking rather than bare function morphemes.

    Under the Basque-like+Latin-abbreviation reading, this would imply: Hand A's highest-frequency words are ALREADY abbreviated-composite forms (Basque stem + case marker + abbreviation collapse). If stripping Layer-2 outers reduces top-10 types to shorter forms, that would partially support the reading. Check: 'daiin' strips to 'daii', 'chol' strips to 'cho', 'chor' strips to 'cho', 'shol' strips to 'sho'. Several strip to identical stems, which is consistent with multiple case-marked forms of the same underlying noun — exactly what agglutinative morphology produces.

    But the Zipf shallowness is a substantive mismatch. Basque's high-frequency vocabulary is dominated by auxiliary verbs (izan, ukan, egon) and common function words (eta, baina, ala) that each account for several percent of tokens. In Hand A, the #1 type 'daiin' accounts for 458/11022 = 4.2% — close to typical Basque top-1 share (~4-6%) — but the distribution decays faster than Basque past that.

    Put differently: Hand A has a HIGH-FREQUENCY HEAD comparable to Basque but a FATTER MID-FREQUENCY REGION. This is consistent with a language whose 'function words' are composite morphological forms rather than bare lexemes.

  NET: MARGINAL under locked rule. The frequency distribution is not a clean Basque match, primarily due to the Zipf exponent. This is a genuine partial inconsistency with the Basque-like reading, not a pure confirmation.

verdict_note: MARGINAL 2/3. Zipf exponent 0.751 is too shallow for Basque (range 0.85-1.15 fails); top-10 share (0.151, at lower bound) and rank-1/rank-10 ratio (4.72) pass. The shallow Zipf replicates H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-NLREF-01's finding that Hand A is more extreme than Latin/Italian on compression measures — a genuinely unusual frequency signature. Hand A's top-10 types are morphologically-complex-looking (mean length 2.80, few 1-glyph types) rather than bare function morphemes typical of Basque. The Basque-like reading is partially inconsistent with Hand A's frequency distribution — this is an honest partial-failure finding, not a confirmation.
findings_for_followup: The Zipf shallowness is replicated from H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-NLREF-01 and represents Hand A's single most persistent statistical anomaly vs natural-language references. A fatter mid-frequency region could indicate: (a) composite forms (e.g., Basque stem+case already collapsed, inflating mid-rank frequency), (b) semantic uniformity driven by a catalogue register (every rank-20-100 word being a plant name, none much more common than another), (c) non-natural origin where no single word dominates.

Follow-up test: if Hand A's HIGH class types are stripped of Layer-2 outers, does the stripped-type Zipf exponent match Basque better than raw? If the scribal-abbreviation framing is right, stripped types should be closer to bare Basque lexemes and show Basque-compatible Zipf.

The top-10 types 'daiin', 'chol', 'chor', 's', 'y', 'shol', 'or', 'ol', 'chy', 'dy' include 'y' and 's' as single-glyph types. 'y' is the top Layer-2 outer in Brain-V's analysis — its appearance as a standalone high-frequency word is anomalous. Either (a) 'y' sometimes functions as a standalone word (like a particle), or (b) 's' and 'y' are noise from tokenisation of short abbreviations. Worth inspection.

For the v2 preprint: this test provides the cleanest evidence that the Basque-like reading is NOT a clean fit. Hand A's Zipf shallowness is a real anomaly that Basque does not explain. The preprint should acknowledge this tension rather than presenting AGGLUTINATIVE-SCREEN-01's 5/5 as definitive.
reasoning: Frequency-distribution shape is a necessary-but-not-sufficient test for language identification. If Hand A is Basque-like, its HIGH class should compress similarly to Basque's high-frequency vocabulary. If it does not, the Basque-like hypothesis is weakened — though any natural language with similar morphological profile (heavy case marking, agglutinative) could pass this test, so passing is not decisive. The test is included because it is cheap, locked, and provides a directional constraint on the family of candidate languages.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed falsification attempt on Basque-like+Latin-abbreviation convergence hypothesis; test 3 of 4
